title: India pharma production hit by propane shortage; vitamin, hormone supply at risk
summary: Indias pharmaceutical industry is facing the risk of medicine shortages as a propane gas crunch forces several manufacturing units to partially or fully shut operations, disrupting production of key drugs including paracetamol, vitamins and hormones.
thbAction: Use the article as an entry point to pitch THB's commercial visibility layer for territory planning, account prioritization, and next-best-action for pharma leadership teams.
contentHtml:
An acute shortage of propane gas used for reactors and steam generation has led to a full or partial shutdown of several pharmaceutical manufacturing in Gujarat and other hubs such as Maharashtra, Telangana and Andhra Pradesh. This has affected the production of a raft of essential medicines such as paracetamol, vitamins and hormones. Industry sources told ET that boiler operations have been severely affected and this may lead to a production loss and shortage of medicines in the next few weeks as companies grapple with existing inventories and negotiate new purchase orders.

India is home to several large scale API manufacturing units that produce a broad spectrum of essential medicines. These facilities are responsible for the bulk synthesis of widely consumed drugs and nutrients, including paracetamol (an analgesic and antipyretic), metformin (a frontline diabetes therapeutic), metronidazole (a critical antibiotic and antiprotozoal), diclofenac and nimesulide (both non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs), ciprofloxacin (a broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic), folic acid and Vitamin C (essential micronutrients vital for public health), and a range of hormones and vitamins that form the backbone of numerous pharmaceutical formulations.

Also Read: Kathua to become India’s antibiotic powerhouse with Rs 600 crore pharma facility

Around 200 manufacturers may halt production within the next 7-10 days, affecting critical medicines, said Dinesh Dua, former chairman, Pharmexcil.

The Indian pharmaceutical industry relies heavily on propane as a primary fuel source to power boilers and sustain the energy-intensive operations involved in API manufacturing.

“The ongoing hostilities have choked LNG import routes, triggering sharp supply shortfalls and an unprecedented surge in gas prices that has left manufacturers scrambling to sustain operations,” said an industry expert.

For an industry already navigating razor-thin margins and intense global competition, the energy crunch threatens to compound existing pressures potentially delaying production timelines and hence resulting in shortage, inflating manufacturing costs, and jeopardizing India’s position as a key supplier of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) to global markets.

He said medicine shortages are likely to be expected. “Paracetamol, syringes, and gloves are among the products likely to be in short supply”.

Bulk Drug Pharmaceutical companies have expressed concerns that the LPG shortage could disrupt the production of life-saving medicines, particularly injectable drugs. “The industry relies heavily on LPG for processes like sterilization, granulation, and boiler operations,” added another expert.

The government has prioritised domestic LPG supply to households and introduced regulations to curb hoarding and black marketing.

“The industry is seeking exemptions similar to those granted during the Covid pandemic, allowing them to continue manufacturing under the essential commodities list. Any disruption to the production carries far-reaching consequences for both domestic patients and international pharmaceutical supply chains,” added the expert.
